observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following courses of action:
The MSWDO and MHO:
Prepare a detailed report of the inventory of supplies under their custody to be submitted to the Accounting Office to record the inventory as an asset;
Maintain stock cards for each classification of supplies to be regularly reconciled with the accounting records (SLCs);
The Municipal Accountant:
Prepare an adjusting entry correcting the erroneous entry to recognize the proper inventory accounts; and
Maintain individual Supplies Ledger Cards for each class of supplies to be regularly reconciled with the MSWDO and MHO’s stock cards.

We recommended the following courses of action:
The Municipal Agricultural Officer:
Submit to the accounting office the list of verified dead animals and other related documents, and request relief from accountability for the said animals, for these to be dropped from the books to arrive at the correct balance of Breeding Stocks;
Determine the current fair value less the cost to sell of the existing Breeding Stocks to be submitted to the Accounting Office for proper recording;
The Municipal Accountant:
Record the biological assets at current fair value less cost to sell, and recognize in the books any gain or loss that may have been incurred, and
Derecognize the amount of P0.125 million representing the value of the recorded buffalo, due to the absence of ownership and control by the LGU over the said assets.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make an adjusting entry to correct the erroneous recording and to recognize immediately as income all succeeding receipts of shares from the charity fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make an adjusting journal entry to correct the previous erroneous entry made to reflect the income that would be recognized under the Government Equity account, and the recorded liability be reduced to its actual balance. Likewise, an adjusting entry also needs to be made to revert the amount of P16,389.75 to the Government Equity account under the General Fund, and furnish the Office of the Auditor with both adjusting entries.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the LGU’s receivable accounts to determine the collectability of the amounts involved. And, whenever necessary, provide an allowance for impairment to properly reflect the fair value of the affected accounts. The provisions for the write-off of dormant accounts in COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated July 17, 2023, should also be considered by the Management.

We recommended that the Management identify the difference between the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds, reconcile both accounts, immediately reflect adjustments, if any, and furnish the Office of the Auditor a copy of the adjustments made.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant comply with the disclosure requirements in the preparation of the financial statements in accordance with IPSASs 19 and 20 to ensure that the entity is transparent about its dealings with related parties and provide users with additional information in understanding the financial statements and aid in the decision-making as well as achieve fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately establish the required SLs for all the control accounts maintained by the LGU, in compliance with the provisions of Section 4(e) and Section 99 of NGAs for LGUs, Volume I, so that all transactions would be properly recorded and monitored.

We recommended that the Management exert more effort in the collection/liquidation of their receivables, and send demand letters to concerned individuals or their heir/s for settlement. For those accounts falling under the dormant receivables as defined by COA Circular No. 2016-005, we recommend that Management request their write-off by completing the documents required under the same COA circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Individually evaluate the existence and validity of aged claims and effect the reversion of claims to the Government Equity account of the General Fund, if warranted; and
Submit the Journal Entry Voucher/s for adjustments made in the books.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the guidelines on the payment of hazard pay as stated in the AO No. 06 s.2023, and discontinue the payment of hazard pay on the days where employees are not exposed to hazards and hardships to avoid disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer:
Determine the cause of the discrepancies between the monthly remittances/refunds against the withholdings, and take the necessary action, whether to remit the difference or refund the same to the employees, whichever is appropriate; and
Ensure that all premium contributions and loan amortizations withheld during the month are remitted in their entirety on or before the tenth day of the following month. Likewise, ensure that all premium contributions, both personal and government shares of the LGU employees, are remitted to the GSIS, to ensure that the former will be able to fully enjoy the benefits provided by the institution.

We recommended the following courses of action:
The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the balances of the Due to Pag-IBIG, Due to PhilHealth, and Due to BIR accounts and, upon determination, remit the balances to the concerned agencies or refund the same to the concerned individuals, whichever is appropriate; and
The Management ensures that all taxes, compulsory contributions and loan repayments withheld during the month are remitted in their entirety on or before the 10th day of the ensuing month.

We recommended the following courses of action:
The Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to determine the status of implementation of the MDF amounting to P1.050 million and furnish the same to the Municipal Accounting Office for monitoring and determination of appropriate action to be undertaken; and
The Municipal Accountant to demand the submission of liquidation reports for the various projects totaling P9.700 million for proper recording.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer enforce the strict collection of the delinquent RPT/SET and formulate an effective strategy or policy to compel the taxpayers to pay their taxes actively and on time pursuant to the provisions of Section 249 of the Local Government Code of the Philippines.

We recommended that the Management fast-track the implementation of the 20 programs and projects funded under the 20% Development Fund to ensure that the constituents can enjoy the benefits that they could derive from the programs and projects aimed at achieving socio-economic development and attaining the desired Municipal objectives. Likewise, monitoring and assessment by the Local Finance Committee should also be undertaken to ensure the timely completion of these projects.
We recommended that the Management implement the identified programs, projects and activities within the planned period of completion to fully achieve its intended purposes.

We recommended that the Management ensure that payroll deductions shall be limited following the order of preference provided in the GAA and ensure that an employee’s net take-home pay shall not be less than the minimum allowed take-home pay of P5,000.00, as provided under the provisions of the General Appropriations Act.
We also recommended that the Management hold the withholding agents responsible for allowing the net take-home pay of the employees to be less than that allowed by the GAA.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Management, through the LGU GFPS, prepare the GAD Agenda and database and use it as the basis for the annual formulation of more gender-responsive PPAs to be included in the LGU GPB pursuant to Section 4.1.C.2.1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.

We recommended that only programs and activities that are responsive to the gender issues identified as a result of the formulation of the GAD Agenda shall be included in the GPB and Accomplishment Reports.

We recommended that Management communicate/inquire with the PWC or other agency offering seminars on GAD, and ensure that the members of the GFPS, its TWG and Secretariat, undergo the capacity training enumerated under Item 4.3 of the PWC-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 to equip them with the knowledge to accomplish their functions properly.

We recommended that the Management pay the other personnel benefits by directly crediting their payroll accounts through an automated payroll system to address the risk of loss/misappropriation and to ensure timely delivery of these other personnel benefits to all employees of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Management require the concerned officials to attach approved payroll with net payments to cash advances for salaries and wages and similar payments and an estimate of expenses for cash advances for operating expenses as a basis for the amount to be granted.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that disbursements for Representation and Transportation Allowance (RATA) are supported with the documentary requirements prescribed under Item 5.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.  Likewise, the Municipal Accountant should not process the disbursement voucher without the required certification issued by the officials/employees concerned.

We recommended that the Management enforce immediate liquidation of cash advances, which are due for liquidations especially those which are long overdue in accordance with Sections 5.7 and 5.8 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and, if warranted, resort to legal remedies to enforce liquidation or settlement in the case of those which are no longer in the service.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive enforce the settlement of the above audit disallowances by issuing demand letters to persons liable with consideration of the solidary liability of the persons named in the Notices of Disallowance issued during prior years; and the submission of the proof of recovery from the insurance coverage.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make an adjusting journal entry to correct the previous erroneous journal entry made so that the income would be recognized under the Government Equity account, and the recorded liability be reduced to its actual balance.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make an adjusting entry to derecognize the liability account Due to Local Economic Enterprise amounting to P140,000.00 and close it to the Government Equity account, to correct the misstatement in the Statement of Financial Position of the LGU.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that interest income from the Trust Fund is recognized in the period it was earned, regardless of the time it was transferred to the General Fund.

We recommended the following courses of action:
 
The Management through the Sangguniang Bayan to formulate implementing guidelines to regulate the financial aid/assistance granted to various individuals to avoid the inequitable allocation of financial aid or assistance to the constituents of the Municipality; and

The Management ensures that the documentary requirements pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD 1445 and DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 2, series of 2014 and DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 11, series of 2019. In particular, the Municipal Accountant should take responsibility for ensuring the completeness of the required documents before a transaction is processed for payment.

observations.recommendation:
We commended  Management  for  the  transparent,  well-documented,
          efficient and  systematic  distribution  of  goods  purchased  and
          donated from benefactors for the relief operations of the  LGU  in
          response to the COVID-19 pandemic. Hence, we recommended that  the
          practice  be  maintained  in  the   future   and   reporting   and
          documentation  be  regularly  submitted  in  accordance  with  COA
          Memorandum No. 2014-009.

We commended the municipal government for its proper  control  and
          usage of its finances, and its good performance on the  collection
          of its revenues to achieve sound fiscal administration. Hence,  we
          recommended that these practices be sustained in the future.

We recommended Management through the Disbursing Officer to submit
          proofs of release of cash to recipients, who failed to affix their
          signatures in the payrolls to duly acknowledge  their  receipt  of
          payments of these expenditures. We also recommended Management  to
          consider using its payroll account for the bonuses and  allowances
          of permanent employees to avoid risk of exposure of large  amounts
          of cash in times of payments of these to employees.

We recommended Management, through the Municipal Treasurer and the
          Municipal Accountant, to ensure that cash advances, other than for
          official  travels,  shall  only  be  granted  to  duly  designated
          Disbursing Officer/s.

We  recommended  Management  to  immediately  demand   for   fund
          utilization  reports  for  completed  PPAs  implemented   by   the
          barangays  through  the  funds   received   from   the   Municipal
          Development Fund. We likewise recommended that aside from ensuring
          that funds are utilized for the  purpose,  there  should  also  be
          validation conducted on the outputs or accomplishments reported by
          the barangays on PPAs to include both civil  works  and  non-civil
          works  projects  and  these  should  have  been  included  in  the
          liquidation documents to be submitted for audit.

We recommended Management,  through  the  Municipal  Planning  and
          Development Council, to determine the plan for unexpended balances
          of funds transferred to barangays. If the excess  funds  were  not
          requested  for  realignments  by  the  barangays  to  other  such
          request is not approved,   the   municipal   government   should
          demand  for  the  refund  of  unexpended  funds  pursuant  to
          Section 4.9 of COA Circular No. 1994-013.

We recommended  the  Management  to  coordinate  with  the  donor-
          agencies to secure the necessary documents of ownership (e.g. MOA,
          registration documents of  vehicle,  deed  of  donation)  and  the
          Municipal Accountant to reflect the necessary  correction  in  the
          books. We  likewise  recommended  the  appraisal  of  the  donated
          properties (if warranted) to facilitate  the  measurement  of  the
          assets.

We recommended Management, through the  Municipal  Accountant,  to
          effect the  necessary  corrections  in  the  books.  Likewise,  we
          recommended Management to coordinate  with  the  supplier  on  the
          processing of its claim for refund of the safeguard duty tax.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant effect the  necessary
          corrections in the books.

We recommended Management through the Municipal Accountant and the
          Municipal Budget Officer to individually  evaluate  the  existence
          and validity of aged  claims  booked  and  earmarked  as  Accounts
          Payable and effect the reversion of claims to  the  unappropriated
          surplus, if warranted.


       Likewise submit the result of evaluation and indicate  as  remarks
          the reason/s for reversion of each  aged  accounts.  In  addition,
          please submit the Journal Entry Voucher/s for adjustments made  in
          the books.

We recommended Management, through the  concerned  officials,  the
          following course of action:

           a.  The  Municipal  Accountant  should  effect   the   necessary
              corrections in the books;


           b. The Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) should regularly
              update the  Accounting  Office  of  instances  of  employee’s
              resignation,  retirement,  end  of  term  or  any  modes   of
              separation of service for proper recognition of claims in the
              books; and


           c. The Municipal Budget Officer should consider to  earmark  the
              accrued payable on terminal leave  benefits  earned  by  each
              employee  even  on  a  staggered  basis  depending   on   the
              availability of funds.

We recommended Management, through the  concerned  personnel,  the
          following courses of action:

           a. The Municipal  Accountant,  in  coordination  with  the  GSIS
              Authorized Agency Officer (AAO), should finalize the schedule
              of loan amortizations and contributions not yet  remitted  to
              GSIS and remit them the soonest possible time. If  there  are
              included  amounts  that  are  not  due  to  GSIS  because  of
              erroneous  or  excessive   withholding   from   salaries   of
              employees, cause the immediate refund of them to employees.


           b. For issues encountered on system  rejection  of  remittances,
              Management, through the AAO, should make a report and resolve
              the matter with GSIS  the  soonest  possible  time  to  avoid
              piling up of unremitted dues to GSIS.

We reiterated the following courses of action:

           a. Management, through the Municipal Treasurer, in  coordination
              with  the  concerned  Punong  Barangays  should  continue  to
              intensify its efforts to account for the unreturned BIR  Form
              16 and to demand for the remittance of collections  therefrom
              from the deputized Barangay Treasurers.


           b. Those Barangay Treasurers who are  still  active  in  service
              should not be issued new CTC booklets until they  have  fully
              remitted collections  therefrom  and  returned/accounted  the
              accountable forms.  Please  advise  the  concerned  barangays
              through their Punong Barangays  and  Barangay  Treasurers  to
              advise their constituents to make their payments of Community
              Taxes to the MTO for the meantime that the collection of CTCs
              is not available in their respective barangays.

We recommended Management, through the Municipal  Accountant,  to
          record correctly future transactions for  monetization  of  leave
          credits  as  Terminal  Leave  Benefits  in  accordance  with  COA
          Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended the Management to ensure claims for monetization of
          unused  leave   credits   shall   be   supported   with   complete
          documentation in accordance with Item 5.14  of  COA  Circular  No.
          2012-001. For noted transactions with deficiencies, please  comply
          with the lacking documents to avoid the  suspension  of  these  on
          audit.

We recommended Management to determine its real  intent  over  the
          issued properties, whether to maintain them as inventoriable items
          of the Municipality (ownership is maintained) or  donate  them  to
          the concerned national agencies. If the ownership is  intended  to
          be  maintained,  the  appropriate   Acknowledgement   Receipt   of
          Equipment (ARE) should be issued to accountable officers over  the
          properties and necessary corrections in the books should  be  done
          by the Accounting Office. Otherwise,  proper  transfer  should  be
          conducted in accordance with Section 381 of R.A. 7160 evidenced by
          Property Transfer Report.

We recommended Management, through the Bids and Awards  Committee,
          to ensure the following in all of LGU procurements:

           a. That the date when  supplier/contractor  signs  the  Purchase
              Order is always indicated;


           b. That the dates when the Notice of Award and Notice to Proceed
              are received by the supplier/contractor are always indicated;
              and


           c.  That  the  Purchase  Order  shall  always  include  complete
              information on the terms of delivery.

We reiterated our previous audit  recommendation  that  the  Local
          Chief  Executive   enforce   settlement   of   the   above   audit
          disallowances by issuing demand letters  to  persons  liable  with
          consideration on the solidary liability of the  persons  named  in
          the Notices of Disallowance issued during prior years.


       In addition, it is requested that available records, documents and
          necessary information (like whereabout of officials named  therein
          or if the transaction is/was a subject of a pending case)  related
          to ND No. 2001-001-101 (2001) be provided to COA for evaluation of
          possible recourse against the persons liable.

We  reiterated  our  prior  year’s  audit   recommendation   that
          Management, through the GAD  Focal  Point  System  (GFPS),  should

observations.recommendation: We recommended management to refrain from its practice of making direct payments of financial assistance to officials/employees of the operating units/ stations of the national government. Instead, aid/ augmentation to the concerned offices/stations’ operation, programs and activities shall be processed by the Municipality on the basis of the former’s request for procurements/ payments of their program/ project/ activity requirements. The Municipality may transfer funds to these concerned offices/stations, if it cannot be dispensed with and in accordance with the budget appropriated by the LGU to their offices/stations. but these shall be accounted as Due from National Government Agencies and the recipient offices/stations have the obligation to liquidate their expenditures to the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed to implement the recommendation by directly processing requested purchase or payment of concerned stations/operating units of national agencies, but with supporting documents similar to the regular government transactions. Charges though will be sourced from appropriations on financial assistance/ donation or appropriations for their respective offices/stations.

We recommended the following courses of action:

The Accounting Office and the MGSO (together with all other concerned offices like the Municipal Engineering Office and the Municipal Agriculturist Office) shall coordinate at all times to reconcile their PPE ledgers (Accounting Office) and property records (GSO) and gradually clear the discrepancies noted. Additions, disposals and losses should be immediately communicated to the MGSO and Accounting Office and reflected in their respective records on the basis of relevant documents evidencing the event or transaction.

Physical Count must be complete and the corresponding RPCPPE must be properly accomplished (e.g. correct valuation and classification) and complete as to the whereabouts/conditions/status of properties.

The Accounting Office should reconstruct its PPE Ledger Cards using the appropriate form as prescribe under MNGAS for LGUs – Volume II (Annex 9) and properly classify properties for easy monitoring, updating and reconciliation of PPE accounts.

We recommended the Accounting Office to reconstruct the CIP account to reflect on-going construction projects and ensure that the reclassification of the accounts to corresponding PPEs shall only be upon issuance of the Certificate of Completion and Acceptance.

We recommended the Accounting Office to make the necessary adjustment in the books to reflect the correction of erroneous journal entry affecting the net equity and liability accounts. 

Also, it is recommended that the proper classification of assets and liabilities in accordance with IPSAS No. 1 must be applied in the presentation of financial statement.

We recommended that management ensure that the expenditures charged under the 20% Development Fund shall only be limited to those specifically provided under the DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-001 and other related policies and shall also be in accordance with its Annual Investment Plan.

We recommended the Accounting Office to effect the necessary corrections in the books as well as to monitor and demand for the liquidation of funds from the recipient-barangays. 

In addition, we recommend the MPDO to still monitor status of implementation of P/A/Ps funded by counter-part funds of the Municipality out of the 20% DF and to report only the status of the Municipality’s 20% DF P/A/Ps in accordance with actual implementation.

We recommended the Municipal Budget Officer to strictly monitor charges from Special Education Fund in accordance with the LSB Resolution authorizing budget and realignments for SEF. Aside from this, any deviations or supplemental to what was appropriated should be backed by relevant LSB authorization.

We recommended the following courses of action:

Enforce settlement from borrowers of unpaid obligations to GSIS thru withholding from their salaries or any available claims from the Municipality (for active in-service borrowers) and send demand letters to retired/ resigned employee-borrowers.

Prior to recommending approval of loans to GSIS, the AAO shall first ensure that loan applicant-employee has sufficient capacity to pay by determining net take home pay in accordance with Section 47 of the 2019 GAA and other similar provisions of subsequent years’ GAA. Include in his/ her assessment if the employee has pending case that might suspend his/ her payment of loans in the future or if an intent to retire or resign was tendered.

Obligations to GSIS should be given preference on deductions from salaries and other claims of employees over other deductions in accordance with Section 47 of the 2019 GAA. 

Issuance of clearance to retiring or resigning employees should be strictly controlled to ensure that an employee intending to exit from service is clear from his/ her liabilities to the Municipality or to other institutions like GSIS, which the former is jointly responsible in accordance with existing laws, regulations or Memorandum of Agreements.

observations.recommendation:
1.8	We recommended that management require:
(1) the complete physical inventory of municipal properties and immediately reconcile the balances of any affected accounts to permit fair presentation of PPE in the financial statements; and
(2) 	maintain Stock and Property Cards, through the MGSO, to properly account the receipt and disposition of PPE items.

3.7	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Engineering Office, MPDO and the Accounting Office to reconcile all on-going projects to ensure that they are recognized in the appropriate Construction in Progress account; and
Accounting Office to update its subsidiary records supporting the transactions booked as completed, and those CIPs for the purpose of ascertaining the existence and propriety of accepted projects.

4.7	We recommended that management:
conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and fund transfers, and determine the concerned debtors; and
request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts and attach required documents prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005.

5.5	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant revert to the unappropriated surplus the accounts payable totaling ₱402,281.71 which have been outstanding for more than two (2) years and for which no valid claims have been established.

5.13	We recommended that management require the Accounting Office to:
Analyze, in coordination with the Treasury Office, the collections held in trust which have remained outstanding for many years whether these may be reverted to the General Fund; and
Analyze and take up the necessary adjustments, duly supported with proper documents, for the misclassified transactions to correct the account balances.
6.3	We recommended that management immediately revert the unutilized balance of PDAF to the national treasury to comply with the decision of the Supreme Court on the matter.

7.5	We recommended that management record all purchases of supplies/materials and medicines to their respective inventory account following the perpetual inventory method, with the exception of those purchased through petty cash. Moreover, inventory control account should be adequately maintained by the General Services Office. Stock cards and property cards for supplies/materials and medicines should be maintained and weekly consolidation of the items issued should be reported using the SSMI to the Municipal Accountant as the basis for the recording of the issuances made thereon. Adherence to Sections 114 and 121 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System is enjoined.

8.7	We recommended that management:
ensure that trust funds are used exclusively to the purpose/s for which they were granted following the provisions of Section 4(3) of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 305(e) and 309(b) of R.A. No. 7160; and
require the settlement of long outstanding receivables to augment the deficient cash under trust fund.

9.6	We recommended that management:
explain the incurrence of negative balances of accounts; and
adhere to the provisions Section 112 of PD No 1445 in the recording of financial transactions and operations in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles and in accordance with pertinent laws and regulations.

10.7	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive enforce settlement of the above audit disallowances which have become final and executory and in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006. Also, the proof of settlements should be simultaneously presented to the office of the auditor for recording and timely issuance of Notice of Settlement of Suspension/Disallowance/Charge (NSSDC).

11.10	We recommended management:
to require/demand the immediate settlement of all long overdue cash advances and if necessary withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees for the settlement thereof;
particularly the Local Chief Executive to request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts to the COA Audit Team Leader and/or Supervising Auditor. The request shall be supported by documents as required under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 on the Guidelines and Procedures on the Write-off of Dormant Receivable Accounts, Unliquidated Cash Advances, and Fund Transfers; and
comply strictly with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Cir. No. 97 – 002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

12.5	We recommended that Management stop the granting of cash advances to municipal officials and employees who are not duly bonded to ensure the truthful disbursement of funds for their intended purposes and the proper safekeeping thereof.

13.5	We recommended that management fast tract the implementation of all on – going and not yet started programmed and funded projects under the 20% MDF to optimize utilization thereof and timely deliver the desired benefits to the intended beneficiaries.

14.7	We recommended that management continuously monitor and promptly implement the programmed and funded projects under the continuing appropriations of 20% Development Fund. Management should also revisit PPAs with remote possibility of implementation especially those which have remained unimplemented for many years and instead reappropriate the funds for PPAs which in its discretion are urgent and most needed.

15.12	We recommended that management ensure that the expenditures charged under the 20% Development Fund shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures to comply with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1.

17.7	We recommended that management:
Require all end user schools to submit to the BAC Secretariat their duly approved Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMPs) for their different programs, activities and projects during the year and ensure that pertinent information/data as provided under Sec. 7.3 of the IRR of RA 9184 are included therein; and
Require the BAC Secretariat to consolidate the submitted PPMPs into an Annual Procurement Plan (APP) and to prepare the required APP for common-use supplies and equipment for submission to DBM using the standard form as set forth by the regulation.

18.7	We recommended that management:
explain the non-existence of LDRRMFIP as basis for its disbursements of prior years’ LDRRMF; and
adhere to all regulations on the use of LDRRMF particularly COA Circular No. 2012-002.
19.4	We recommended that management monitor the implementation of all budgeted projects, programs and activities to ensure their full implementation.

20.7	We recommended that Management strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 271 (3)(d) of the Local Government Code and remit promptly the barangay shares in the municipal real property and community tax collections to augment the finances of the affected local government units.

21.10	We strongly recommended that the Municipality stop the practice of paying RATA in advance to conform to the provisions of Section 88 of P.D. 1445 and submit the lacking DTRs and minutes of regular sessions to support the propriety of its payment.

22.7	We recommended that management require the appropriate personnel to reevaluate the configuration of the E-TRACS in order to maximize the benefits that could be derived therefrom. Management may also confer with other LGUs to replicate best practices in the maintenance of their systems.

23.8	We commended management for its immediate action and effort in complying with the previous year’s findings and recommendations on internal control procedures. Further, it was recommended that management continuously adhere with regulations to ensure that the correct amount of taxes, fees and charges are properly ascertained, assessed and collected. If possible, the number of establishments subjected to examination may be further increased to uncover more deficiencies in the payment of business taxes.

24.3	We recommended that management ensure that only relevant characteristics and operational requirements are indicated in the documents for the procurement of goods and strictly adhere to the provisions of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184, otherwise known as the Government Procurement Reform Act.

25.4	We recommended that Management:
further strengthen its internal control by ensuring that all financial reports and forms are properly and completely accomplished to guaranty the propriety and regularity of its transactions; and
strictly adhere with the provisions of the Volume II of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for LGUs.

26.5	We recommended that management continuously comply with the provisions of R.A. No. 9710 including its Implementing Rules and Regulations and to submit a GAD Accomplishment Report for validation by the audit team.

27.7	We recommended that management require the Accounting Office to reconcile records with the BIR, GSIS and Pag-IBIG and immediately remit the unremitted withheld taxes, contributions and loan payments.
28.5	We recommended that management submit to DOH the liquidation papers pertaining to the amount of P1,272,483.42 already adjusted in the books of the municipality.

observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding Nos. 7 and 8, Part II, pages 54 and 55 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management provide proper appropriation in every budget year for the wages of contractuals/job orders under the PS account Salaries and Wages-Casual/Contractual who are performing regular agency functions and specific vital activities or services which cannot be provided by the regular or permanent staff of the agency and refrain from providing lump sum appropriation under the MOOE account Other Professional Services in order to properly classify expenses during budgeting and also during reporting.
observations.managementAction: Management committed to comply with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant require the submission of complete documents that support each transaction before payment by the Municipal Treasurer in order to avoid suspensions in audit. Likewise, she shall see to it that claims are not irregular, excessive, unnecessary and extravagant as prohibited by laws, rules and regulations to pass the transaction in audit.
observations.managementAction: Management committed to comply with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management prepare and submit to the Auditor the Program of Works, PERT CPM and GANTT Chart of projects together with other supporting documents for claims of wages of contracted laborers to facilitate evaluation of the extent of completion if payment really commensurate accomplishment.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer committed to attach all the required supporting documents as recommended.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant require the completeness of the claims of casuals and job orders for every 15th and 30th claim for wages to ensure that payments to them are commensurate to the services they rendered so that propriety thereof could be ascertained. We also recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer assist the casual and contractual personnel to submit Accomplishment Reports as basis in computing wages, certify these forms as to veracity and correctness and compute allowable daily attendance as basis for payment. The Local Chief Executive should also attach on all payrolls for wages his certification that the employment/hiring is still within the PS limitation to serve as an indicator that PS is being utilized efficiently and effectively within the limits set by law since these wages should be properly appropriated under Salaries and Wages-Casual/Contractual.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal already required the casual and job order personnel to submit their individual accomplishment report upon claiming their wages in 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended management that all requests for the procurement of medicines be supported with a certification of the requisitioning officer that the medicines fall within and conform to the PNDF Volume 1.
observations.managementAction: Management complied with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF should be submitted monthly to the audit team in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 specifically on the reporting guidelines for the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF).
observations.managementAction: Management complied with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management follow the rates prescribed under Sections 7.2 and 7.2.2 of DBM-DOH Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2012 in computing the Hazard Pay of Public Health Workers to prevent the unnecessary use of government funds and properties.
observations.managementAction: Management and PHW employees have agreement that the rate of their Hazard Pay will still 10% of their monthly rate however, if there will be saving at the end of the year, they will give the differential of their hazard pay.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management continue to strategize and expedite the implementation of government projects and programs by maximizing the use of government resources in coordination with other support agencies, submit liquidation and accomplishment report to the grantor agency as required by pertinent Memorandum of Agreements and comply with the existing laws, rules and regulations on the prosecution of projects to ensure that government moneys are used economically, efficiently and effectively in the delivery of public services.
observations.managementAction: Most of the national government projects were implemented except those funds were released late.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Accounting Office verify subsidiary ledgers before certifying the disbursement voucher for the grant of cash advances. This will also serve as reminder to other signatories to the voucher to disapprove proposed additional cash advances, if found not in compliance with Section 4.1.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002.
observations.managementAction: Some officials and employees were still allowed to avail additional cash advances even if their previous cash advances were not yet fully liquidated.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 6, Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 271 (3)(d) of the Local Government Code and remit promptly the barangay and Provincial shares in the municipal real property and community tax collections to augment the finances of the affected local government units. 

The audit team further recommended that the Municipal Treasury release the shares of the barangays by preparing the check payments quarterly and obliged the Barangays to receive their shares so that it will form part of the latters’ income during the same period.
observations.managementAction: Unreleased RPT shares as at December 31, 2016 amounted to P4,329,446.44.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No.10, Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Accounting Office and MPDO committed that the monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF should be submitted to the audit team in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 specifically on the reporting guidelines for the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund.
observations.managementAction: No action taken.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 22, Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Accounting Office and the General Service Office reconcile their general ledger and RPCPPE to clear the discrepancies between the two records in compliance with the provisions of the Handbook on Property and Supply Management System.
During the exit conference, the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal General Service Officer committed to identify the unreconciled balance and make the necessary adjustments and/or recording.

We recommended to Management the following:
Strictly comply with the provisions of pertinent accounting and auditing rules and regulations on the preparation of bank reconciliation statements particularly COA Circular No. 96-11 dated October 2, 1996, the Manual on New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units and also the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual (GAAM) Volume III;
The Municipal Accounting Office make necessary adjustments for all reconciling items presented in the bank reconciliation statements, such as, debit memoranda (DMs) and error in recording during the period these were identified in order to reflect the correct balance of the Cash in Bank account in any accounting period; and
The Accounting Office coordinate closely with the Treasury Office to determine the status and nature of bank accounts to keep these updated and thereby be guided on the fair presentation thereof in the Financial Statements.
The Municipal Accountant stated that the adjustments will be reflected in 2017.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer issue official receipt for all fund transfers from other government agencies and donations received from private individuals. The Liquidating Officer shall record in his corresponding cashbook the cash or check collection upon receipt and as deposit upon actual deposit in compliance with the sound recording of government transactions and with the provisions of COA Circular No. 92-382 to ensure that all received funds are duly accounted for and their disposition are properly monitored.
The collecting officer complied with the recommendation in 2017.

We recommended that Management demand the remittance of the P10,450.00 value of cash tickets from the former field collectors in compliance with the provisions of Section 62 (D) of the Local Treasury Operations Manual and Section 33c of COA Memorandum No. 2013-004.
During the exit conference, the Municipal Treasure stated that part of the said unremitted cash tickets were already collected and those collectors that did not yet pay their said unremitted cash tickets were being located and demand letter will be issued to them.

We recommended that the concerned management officials and employees liquidate and settle their cash advances in accordance with Sections 5.7 and 5.8 of COA Circular No. 97-002. Management may resort to legal remedies to enforce liquidation or settlement in the case of those no longer in the service.
Management stated that they issued demand letter to the accountable officer requiring them to liquidate their unliquidated cash advances.

We recommended that the Accounting Office verify subsidiary ledgers before certifying the disbursement voucher for the grant of cash advances. This will also serve to remind other signatories to the voucher to disapprove proposed additional cash advances if found not in compliance with Section 4.1.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002.
The Municipal Accountant stated that he is issuing a certificate disclosing that the person acquiring new cash advance has no unliquidated cash advances. However, as reasoned out by the other officials and employees, there were times that they cannot avoid to incur additional cash advances while they still have unliquidated cash advances due to some situations like when they have unliquidated cash advances for a certain project or program and this program is still on-going and then they have scheduled training/seminar that they need to incur additional cash advance for such training/seminar.

We recommended that Management stop the granting of cash advances to municipal officials and employees who are not duly bonded to ensure the truthful disbursement of funds for their intended purposes and for their proper safekeeping.
Management stated that they already avoid giving cash advances to unbonded officers or employees in compliance with the recommendation.

We recommended management to identify the source of the unreconciled balance between the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds through reconciliation of the accounts and effect immediately the adjustments.
The Municipal Accountant stated that they will make the necessary adjustment in 2017.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive enforce settlement of the above audit disallowances that are final and executory and in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006. Also, the proof of settlements should be simultaneously presented to the office of the auditor for recording and timely issuance of Notice of Settlement of Suspension/Disallowance/Charge (NSSDC).
The Municipal Treasurer committed to furnish copy of the receipt of the payment made by Ms. Valdez to the Auditor for the issuance of NSSDC. The Management further committed to send demand letter to those respective accountable persons and if the accountable persons were already dead or insolvent, the management will request for the write-off of these disallowances.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 271 (3)(d) of the Local Government Code and remit promptly the barangay and Provincial shares in the municipal real property and community tax collections to augment the finances of the affected local government units. We further recommended the adjustment of the negative balances of the accounts.
Management stated during the exit conference that the Barangays withdraw their RPT shares if they wanted to, usually monthly. However, if the barangays will not withdraw their shares, it is also up to them.
The Audit team enjoined that the Management shall release the RPT shares of the barangays quarterly and not wait for the barangays to withdraw them.

We recommended that the municipality stop the practice of paying representation and transportation allowances (RATA) in advance to conform with the provisions of Section 88 of P.D. 1445.
Management committed to comply with the recommendation.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant require complete documentation to government payments to ensure that municipal payments are properly received and documented in settlement of obligations incurred in consonance with Section 4(6) of PD 1445.
Management stated that they did not notice that the receipt was only provisional and they committed to secure the official receipt.

We recommended that Management immediately stop the practice of forging or falsifying signatures and official stamps of prospective suppliers to prevent the occurrence of irregular transactions. The Municipality should enter into contract with legal and bonafide dealers to obtain the price most advantageous to the government as well as save also on increments a non-legitimate supplier might add on the cost of supplies on other price quotations.
Management committed to comply with the recommendation.

We recommended that management implement all programmed projects during the year which were legislated and approved to allow early utilization by the beneficiaries thereof. Likewise, monitoring and assessment by the Local Finance Committee should also be undertaken to ensure timely completion of these projects.
Management stated that some PPAs of the 20% DF were not implemented due to bad weather and delay in bidding process.

We recommended that Management provide proper appropriation in every budget year for the wages of contractuals/job orders under the PS account Salaries and Wages-Casual/Contractual who are performing regular agency functions and specific vital activities or services which cannot be provided by the regular or permanent staff of the agency and refrain from providing lump sum appropriation under the MOOE account Other Professional Services in order to properly classify expenses during budgeting and also during reporting.
Management committed to comply with the recommendation.

We recommended that drivers of municipal vehicles as well as construction and heavy equipment prepare the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption and Report of Official Travels pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 77-61, submit them monthly to COA and at the same time, attach them to disbursement vouchers for the payment of fuel and lubricants.
Management committed to comply with the recommendation.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant require the submission of complete documents that support each transaction before payment by the Municipal Treasurer in order to avoid suspensions in audit. Likewise, she shall see to it that claims are not irregular, excessive, unnecessary and extravagant as prohibited by laws, rules and regulations to pass the transaction in audit.
Management committed to comply with the recommendation.

We recommended that Management prepare and submit to the Auditor the Program of Works, PERT CPM and GANTT Chart of projects together with other supporting documents for claims of wages of contracted laborers to facilitate evaluation of the extent of completion if payment really commensurate accomplishment.
The Municipal Engineer committed to attach all the required supporting documents as recommended.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant require the completeness of the claims of casuals and job orders for every 15th and 30th claim for wages to ensure that payments to them are commensurate to the services they rendered so that propriety thereof could be ascertained.
We also recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer assist the casual and contractual personnel to submit Accomplishment Reports as basis in computing wages, certify these forms as to veracity and correctness and compute allowable daily attendance as basis for payment. The Local Chief Executive should also attach on all payrolls for wages his certification that the employment/hiring is still within the PS limitation to serve as an indicator that PS is being utilized efficiently and effectively within the limits set by law since these wages should be properly appropriated under Salaries and Wages-Casual/ Contractual.
Management already required the casual and job order personnel to submit their individual accomplishment report upon claiming their wages in 2017.

We recommended management that all requests for the procurement of medicines be supported with a certification of the requisitioning officer that the medicines fall within and conform to the PNDF Volume 1.
Management stated that they were attaching certification that their procured medicines fall within and conform with the PNDF, however, there were times that they forgot to attach the required certification. They committed that in 2017 they will comply with the recommendation.

We recommended that the monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF should be submitted monthly to the audit team in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 specifically on the reporting guidelines for the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF).
Management committed to comply with the recommendation.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee submit on or before January 31 of each year the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment to the Accountant who shall conduct the necessary reconciliation and to the Auditor who shall ascertain the existence and proper valuation of all PPEs to ensure that all municipal properties are presented at fair value in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Management submitted the RPCPPE as at December 31, 2016.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the concerned management officials and employees liquidate and settle their cash advances in accordance with Sections 5.7 and 5.8 of COA Circular No. 97-002. Management may resort to legal remedies to enforce liquidation or settlement in the case of those no longer in the service.
observations.managementAction: Management manage to lessen the unliquidated cash advances to to P3,437,457.67.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management identify the source of the unreconciled balance between the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds through reconciliation and effect immediately the needed adjustments.
observations.managementAction: Management lessen the unreconciled balance to P1,303,393.87 as at December 31, 2016.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 8, Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the collecting officer deposit intact all the collections daily or not later that the next banking day in compliance with the provision of Section 28 of the COA Circular No. 92-382 to avoid misusage of cash on hand.
observations.managementAction: All collections as December 31, 2016 were deposited on the next working day in 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive enforce settlement of the above audit disallowances that are final and executory and in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006. Also, all proof of settlements should be simultaneously presented to the office of the auditor for recording and timely issuance of Notice of Settlement of Suspension/Disallowance/Charge (NSSDC).
observations.managementAction: As per accounting record, amount of P158,523.80 were collected in 2016. However, additional ND was issued, leaving total ND of P1,333,341.29 at year end.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 9, Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management ensure that the list of taxpayers showing the names and the amounts due and collectibles based on the Real Property Tax Account Register (RPTAR)/Taxpayer’s Index Card is accurate to provide a reliable basis in the setting up of deferred and receivable accounts of RPT and SET as prescribed in Section 20 of the MNGAS, Volume I in order to avoid incurrence of negative balances at year-end.

We further recommended that the Accounting office effect the necessary adjustments in the books of the municipality to correct the negative balances of the affected accounts.
observations.managementAction: Accounting Office made the necessary adjustments.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management discontinue the practice of paying the year end benefits of officials and employees from the unexpended allotments of MOOE in compliance to Section 336 of the Local Government Code.  Otherwise, the CRA would not be allowed in audit due to the resulting violation of cardinal laws, rules and regulations in its disbursement and in order to ensure that government moneys are protected and used for their intended public purposes.
observations.managementAction: Management did not pay unauthorized benefits to officers and employees in 2016.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management utilize appropriately the Special Education Fund (SEF) and comply with the rules set under Section 1 of RA 5447 and Section 272 of the Local Government Code (RA 7160).  Any improper utilization of the SEF shall be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: Management already stop charging improper expenses from SEF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the concerned officials and employees return the irregularly disbursed funds and for the management to follow the guidelines set by DBM LBC No. 103 in computing the amount of RATA to be paid to prevent irregular disbursements of government funds.
observations.managementAction: The amount of P57,825.00 was refunded in 2016. In 2016, management paid excessive RATA amounting to P13,500.00, however this amount was fully refunded in 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management stop the practice of charging the wages of the cemetery cleaner from the 20% Development Fund and return to the Fund all previous charges for the year by taking these expenses from the appropriations of the Municipal General Services Office (MGSO) which is the appropriate cost and responsibility center. Rectify future AIPs by reclassifying these wages to be charged against the MGSO budget. The cost of constructing the cemetery shed should be disbursed outside of the Fund and should also be restituted to the Fund. These unused returned funds to the 20% DF should form part of continuing appropriations at the end of the year to be reprogrammed for development projects in the ensuing year 2016 in accordance with the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1.
observations.managementAction: Management stop charging same nature of expense from 20% DF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality disburse the BAC and TWG honoraria to be funded from legal sources as enumerated in Sections 3.1.1 of Budget Circular No. 2007-3. Otherwise, Management should resort to Section 3.1.4 of same Budget Circular in accordance with Sections 3.3 and 3.5 thereof. We also recommended that Management attach claims for honoraria with documents of successfully bidded projects and such other papers required by COA Circular No. 2012-001. Likewise, Management should stop granting honoraria to observers attending the bidding process as required in Budget Circular No. 2004-5a.
observations.managementAction: Management complied with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Management committed to submit the documentary requirements of the aforementioned disbursements in accordance to the provisions of Section 13.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to avoid the suspension in audit of the pertinent transactions.
observations.managementAction: Necessary supporting documents were submitted in 2016.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management stop granting honoraria to the members of the Local Finance Committee and to any other official in relation to the planning of the financial affairs of the local government in consonance to Section 2.1 and 2.2 of DBM Budget Circular No. 2007-2 and Section 95 of RA 7160. Refund of previous claims is thereby enjoined.
observations.managementAction: Management complied with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management follow the rates prescribed under Sections 7.2 and 7.2.2 of DBM-DOH Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2012 in computing the Hazard Pay of Public Health Workers to prevent the unnecessary use of government funds and properties.
observations.managementAction: Management and PHW employees have agreement that the rate of their Hazard Pay will still 10% of their monthly rate however, if there will be saving at the end of the year, they will give the differential of their hazard pay.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the procuring entity stop the practice of splitting purchase requisitions/contracts in compliance with the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA No. 9184, to promote effective competition and transparency in the procurement process and obtain fair and equitable contract for the best interest of the government.
observations.managementAction: Management avoided splitting of their procurement transactions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management implement all programmed projects during the year these were legislated and approved to allow early utilization by the beneficiaries thereof. Likewise, monitoring and assessment by the Local Finance Committee should also be undertaken to ensure timely completion of these projects.
observations.managementAction: Some of the 2015 20% DF PPAs were still not fully implemented in 2016. 50% of PPAs under 2016 20% DF were not implemented as at December 31, 2016.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 15, Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management continue to strategize and expedite the implementation of government projects and programs by maximizing the use of government resources in coordination with other support agencies, submit liquidation and accomplishment report to the grantor agency as required by pertinent Memorandum of Agreements and comply with the existing laws, rules and regulations on the prosecution of projects to ensure that government moneys are used economically, efficiently and effectively in the delivery of public services.
observations.managementAction: Most of the national government projects were implemented except those funds were released late.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Accounting Office adhere with the proforma journal entries in recording the setting up and replenishment of the Petty Cash Fund by implementing the Imprest System as required by the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs to ensure compliance with the established rule.
observations.managementAction: Management complied with the recommend-ation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management prepare separate disbursement vouchers for recurring expenses and pay them through the issuance of checks, limit the amounts charged to Petty Cash to P 1,000.00 per voucher pursuant to Section 48 of the New Government Accounting System and effect replenishment once 75% of the fund was spent as stated in Section 4.3.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 to prevent irregular disbursements of government funds.
observations.managementAction: Management complied with the recommend-ation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management procure goods and services in consonance with the approved Annual Procurement Plan and resort only to emergency purchases in cases of extreme danger to life and property.  Direct payment to supplier is also encouraged as a result of compliance to the regular procurement rules and regulations to obtain the price which is most advantageous to the government.
observations.managementAction: Management complied with the recommend-ation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Accounting Office committed to strictly adhere with the provisions of COA Circular 2009-006 on the submission of accounts for post-audit. Further, administrative and/or criminal action may be initiated by the Local Chief Executive in case of unjustified failure to comply by subordinate officials as provided under said circular.
observations.managementAction: Management complied with the recommend-ation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the management committed to comply with the required completeness of documentary requirements in every transaction as mandated by PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 to ensure the validity and legality of government transactions.
observations.managementAction: Some of 2016 transactions were still not support with all the necessary attachments.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 18, Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management comply with the necessary documentary requirements as mandated by Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to ensure the validity and necessity of Hazard Pay transactions.
observations.managementAction: Management complied with the recommend-ation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended management that all requests for the procurement of medicines be supported with a certification of the requisitioning officer that the medicines fall within and conform to the PNDF Volume 1.
observations.managementAction: Some procurements were supported with the required certification but some were also not supported with the required certification.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 21, Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the municipal government furnish copy of contracts, purchase orders and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor for review and evaluation as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001. Further, administrative disciplinary action shall be imposed in case of unjustified failure of the officials and employees concerned to comply with the requirements as provided under the said Circular.
observations.managementAction: Management complied with the recommendation

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management ensure that only relevant characteristics and operational requirements be indicated in relevant documents for the procurement of goods and strictly adhere to the provisions of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184, otherwise known as the Government Procurement Reform Act.
observations.managementAction: Management complied with the recommendation
